返回本批次

巴黎如何将汽车换成自行车，并改造其街道

How Paris swapped cars for bikes – and transformed its streets

www.theguardian.com 作者 vrganj 分数 50 评论 10 2026/04/06 17:44
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 10 年前，当 Corentin Roudaut 搬到巴黎时，他非常害怕骑自行车。这位 IT 开发人员在雷恩上学时曾骑自行车到处走走，但对熙熙攘攘的法国首都感到不知所措。汽车无处不在。骑自行车的人几乎没有任何保护。但是，当当局在他位于第 11 区的家附近的伏尔泰大道上划出一条隔离自行车道的空间后，鲁多就回到了两轮通勤，并且没有回头。他现在是自行车运动组织 Paris en Selle 的志愿者，并惊奇地看着这座城市摆脱了以汽车为中心的声誉。“这是一个开始缓慢的过程，但在过去 10 年里确实加速了，”鲁多说。 “至少在城市的某些地方，我们拥有一个开始安全且基本完整的[自行车]网络。” 自 2014 年安妮·伊达尔戈 (Anne Hidalgo) 担任市长以来，巴黎开始了一场伟大的变革，种植了 155,000 棵树，增加了数百公里的自行车道，将 300 条学校街道设为步行道，并禁止汽车驶入塞纳河沿岸。停车位已变成绿地以及咖啡馆和酒吧的露台。越来越少的家长担心孩子步行上学时被碾压。伊达尔戈在担任市长 12 年后，于 3 月 29 日星期日卸任。现在，随着各国政府取消绿色政策，她为使这座城市变得更加宜居所做的努力已成为欧洲进步城市的典范。巴黎新市长伊曼纽尔·格雷瓜尔 (Emmanuel Grégoire) 在市政选举的正式结果公布后，骑马来到市政厅。图片来源：Kenzo Tribouillard/AFP/Getty Images让街道更安全的努力占用了汽车空间，引发了驾车者的直接反对，而关于向SUV司机收取更多停车费和增加学校街道步行街的全民公投却以低得令人不安的投票率获胜。在上个月的市政选举之前，右翼共和党市长候选人拉奇达·达蒂(Rachida Dati)批评公共空间的混乱“引发焦虑”，尽管她没有提议撤销中央政策。上周接受《卫报》采访时，伊达尔戈表示，城市河岸步行化建设是“一场艰苦的战斗”，但现在人们不想再回去了。“今天，有几代孩子在那里不认识汽车。当你告诉他们时，人们会说‘哇！’”她说。专家表示，该市异常严格的行政边界使这种转变变得更容易，这使得通勤郊区在交通方面的发言权比其他首都要少，而且前几任市长奠定的基础。但仍然需要勇气来推动那些给驾车者带来不便的政策，同时带来共同的社会和环境效益。2016年，塞纳河沿岸禁止汽车通行，作为减少污染努力的一部分，这也让骑自行车的人感到更安全。照片：pawel.gaul/Getty Images 仍有更多工作要做，但迄今为止的变化“非常棒”，在巴黎长大并经常回国的伦敦帝国理工学院环境流行病学家奥黛丽·德纳泽尔 (Audrey de Nazelle) 说。她记得，当骑自行车还很少见的时候，如果你骑着自行车遇到其他人，“你可以一起去喝咖啡”。“世界其他地方缺少的是勇气，”她说。 “市长们可能会说：‘这是我留下遗产的机会’，但大多数人都不敢。” 上个月的一份报告发现，巴黎是 2010 年至 2024 年间两种有毒空气污染物显着减少的 19 个全球城市之一，尽管该名单还包括少数城市政治进步程度较低的邻近城市。同期，布鲁塞尔和华沙的细颗粒污染下降速度更快，而伦敦的二氧化氮下降速度更快。柏林，去年开放市内新建的一段高速公路，并投票取消了 23 条主要街道的 30 公里/小时限速，骑自行车的比例仍然高于巴黎。 曾居住在巴黎的多特蒙德工业大学交通研究员朱利奥·马蒂奥利 (Giulio Mattioli) 表示，巴黎并非例外，而是起点较低，赶上了许多其他城市。他说：“条件已经具备，你只需要修建一些自行车道，人们就会使用它们。”在Covid-19大流行期间，欧洲各地的城市见证了自行车和自行车友好型基础设施的蓬勃发展，但由于政治右倾和阴谋论的出现而遭受挫折，这些阴谋论出乎意料地针对诸如在步行范围内提供便利设施等想法。尽管从根本上转变为“15分钟城市”，但广阔的郊区仍被汽车占据，并被繁忙的环路所隔断。曾在伊达尔戈及其前任手下任职的前副市长让·路易斯·米西卡(Jean-Louis Missika)对智囊团 Terra Nova 的分析表示，改造环绕城市的环城大道对于使巴黎成为后汽车大都市至关重要。“只要这条 35 公里的高速公路继续环绕巴黎，大巴黎巴黎大都市仍将是想象中的虚构，是一个缺乏城市现实的行政建筑，”他写道。 “因为一座大都市不能通过在居民之间竖起围墙来建造。”

Original Summary

  1. When Corentin Roudaut moved to Paris 10 years ago, he was too scared to cycle. The IT developer had biked everywhere as a student in Rennes but felt overwhelmed by the bustling French capital. Cars were everywhere. Cyclists had almost no protection.But once authorities carved out space for a segregated bike lane on Boulevard Voltaire near his home in the 11th arrondissement, Roudaut returned to the two-wheel commute and did not look back.He now volunteers with Paris en Selle, a cycling campaign group, and has watched with wonder as the city has shaken off its car-centric reputation.“It was a process that started slow and really accelerated in the last 10 years,” Roudaut said. “At least in some parts of the city, we have a [cycle] network that is starting to be safe and pretty much complete.”Paris has embarked on a grand transformation since Anne Hidalgo became mayor in 2014, planting 155,000 trees, adding several hundred kilometres of bike lanes, pedestrianising 300 school streets and banning cars from the banks of the Seine.Parking spots have been turned into green spaces and terraces for cafes and bars. Fewer parents have to fear their child being run over when they walk to school.Hidalgo left office on Sunday 29 March after 12 years as mayor, and now her fight to make the city more livable has been held up as an example for progressive European cities as national governments roll back green policies.Paris’s new mayor, Emmanuel Grégoire, rode to the town hall after the official results of the municipal elections. Photograph: Kenzo Tribouillard/AFP/Getty Images“When people ask me if I have any advice, I say don’t be afraid of being ambitious,” said Roudaut, who last year welcomed a delegation of Green politicians from Germany trying to understand why Paris was doing what Berlin could not.Even though Hidalgo achieved only part of her plan, he added, “everybody’s saying: ‘Look at what Paris has done, it’s so amazing.’”Parisians do not all feel the same. Efforts to make streets safer have taken space away from cars, sparking direct opposition from motorists, while referendums on charging SUV drivers more to park and pedestrianising more school streets were won with troublingly low turnouts.Before last month’s municipal elections, Rachida Dati, the mayoral candidate for the rightwing Les Républicains, criticised the chaos in public space as “anxiety-inducing”, though she stopped short of proposing to undo central policies.In a wide-ranging interview with the Guardian last week, Hidalgo said pedestrianising the city’s riverbanks had been “a tough battle” but now that it had happened people did not want to go back.“Today there are generations of children who have not known cars there. People say ‘wow!’ when you tell them,” she said.Experts say the transition was made easier by the city’s unusually tight administrative boundaries, which give commuter suburbs less say over its transport than in other capitals, as well as groundwork laid by previous mayors.But still courage was needed to push through policies that inconvenienced motorists while introducing shared social and environmental benefits.Cars were banned from the banks of the Seine in 2016 as part of efforts to bring down pollution, which also helped cyclists feel safer. Photograph: pawel.gaul/Getty ImagesMore could still be done but the changes so far are “fabulous”, said Audrey de Nazelle, an environmental epidemiologist at Imperial College London who grew up in Paris and returns frequently. She remembers when cycling was so rare “you could go and have coffee together” if you ran into someone else on a bike.“What’s missing in the rest of the world is courage,” she said. “Mayors could say: ‘This is my opportunity [to leave a] legacy,’ but most will not dare.”Paris is one of 19 global cities that achieved remarkable reductions in two toxic air pollutants between 2010 and 2024, a report last month found, although the list also includes a handful of neighbouring capitals with less progressive urban politics.Fine-particle pollution fell faster in Brussels and Warsaw over the same period, while nitrogen dioxide fell faster in London.Berlin, which last year opened a new stretch of motorway inside the city and voted to scrap 30km/h speed limits on 23 main streets, still has a higher share of cyclists than Paris.Rather than being exceptional, Paris has caught up with many other cities from a lower starting point, said Giulio Mattioli, a transport researcher at the Technical University of Dortmund, who used to live in Paris. “The conditions were there already, you just needed to make some bike lanes and people would use them,” he said.Cities across Europe saw a boom in cycling and bike-friendly infrastructure during the Covid-19 pandemic but have suffered setbacks amid a political shift to the right and the emergence of conspiracy theories that have unexpectedly taken aim at ideas such as having amenities within walking distance.While Paris proper has undergone a radical shift to becoming a “15-minute city”, the extensive suburbs are still dominated by cars and are cut off by a busy ring road.Analysis for the thinktank Terra Nova by Jean-Louis Missika, a former deputy mayor who served under Hidalgo and her predecessor, said transforming the Boulevard Périphérique that surrounds the city was essential to making Paris a post-car metropolis.“As long as this 35km motorway continues to encircle Paris, the Greater Paris metropolis will remain a figment of the imagination, an administrative construct devoid of urban reality,” he wrote. “Because a metropolis cannot be built by erecting walls between its inhabitants.”

评论（默认中文）

zemike 2026/04/07 00:02
随着时间的推移，人们会越来越觉得这些变化让巴黎变得更加伟大。这将类似于阿姆斯特丹和其他城市所取得的进展。一场缓慢而有意识地对抗以汽车为中心的基础设施的斗争，这不是魔法，而是缓慢而稳定的进展。
As time goes by, people will feel like these changes are more and more what makes Paris great. It will be similar to how progress happened for Amsterdam and other cities. A slow conscious fight against car centric infrastructure, it’s not magic, it’s slow steady progress.
在 HN 中查看
Zopieux 2026/04/07 01:03
当地人已经知道并相应地投票。

不幸的是，使用汽车替代品被认为是“觉醒”和左脑思维，因此亿万富翁拥有的媒体（即大多数法国电视和媒体）试图强调这在某种程度上是不好的，使政策进展不必要地缓慢和困难。

Locals already know and vote accordingly.

Unfortunately it is considered "woke" and left-brained to use alternatives to cars, therefore billionaire-owned media (i.e. most of French TV and press) tries to hammer that this is somehow bad, making policy progress needlessly slow and difficult.

在 HN 中查看
throw-the-towel 2026/04/07 01:54
我只是希望有人能教那些该死的骑自行车的人在红灯时停下来。目前来看，他们似乎完全没有能力做到这一点，但仍然全速冲锋。没有更多人受伤真是奇迹。
I just wish somebody would teach the damn cyclists to stop at red lights. As of now, they seem absolutely incapable of doing this, and charge ahead at full speed regardless. It's a miracle there's not more injuries.
在 HN 中查看
Glawen 2026/04/07 02:20
当您等待绿灯时，您与汽车同时启动。汽车似乎无法跟在骑自行车的人后面，并且感觉有必要不惜一切代价超越他们。 作为一个骑自行车的人，在没有人的情况下红灯起步感觉更安全，然后你就可以领先于汽车
When you wait for green you start at the same time as cars. Cars seem incapable of staying behind a cyclist and feel the need to overtake them at all costs. It feel much safer as a cyclist to start at red light when there is noone, you then have a head start with cars
在 HN 中查看
vrganj 2026/04/07 02:48
这是一个基础设施工程问题，而不是一个道德问题。

荷兰通过荷兰环岛解决了这个问题： https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XhqTc_wx5EU

This is an infrastructure engineering problem, not a moral one.

The Netherlands solves this with Dutch Roundabouts: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XhqTc_wx5EU

在 HN 中查看
lbreakjai 2026/04/07 04:41
很容易成为我最不喜欢的基础设施。视频中的那个没问题，因为道路和自行车道之间有一整辆车的长度，所以你处于完全垂直的位置，可以很好地看到自行车。

在许多其他地方，空间要小得多，这使得自行车交通几乎直接位于您过马路时的盲点。

例如，这里： https://www.google.com/maps/@52.3911559,4.6253401,3a,75y,110...

这真的很难预料，因为当你开车时，你可能没有完全无障碍的视野，特别是因为骑自行车的人可能来得很快。

https://www.aviewfromthecyclepath.com/2014/05/the-best-round...

Easily my least favourite piece of infrastructure. The one on the video is alright because you have a full car's length between the road and the cycle path, so you are at a full perpendicular with good visibility over the bikes.

In plenty of other places, the space is much smaller, which puts the bike traffic almost directly in your blind spot as you cross.

For example, here: https://www.google.com/maps/@52.3911559,4.6253401,3a,75y,110...

It's really hard to anticipate as you may not have full unobstructed visibility as you drive around, especially since cyclists can come quite fast.

https://www.aviewfromthecyclepath.com/2014/05/the-best-round...

在 HN 中查看
vrganj 2026/04/07 04:57
是的，全车长度是一个关键的设计特征。没有它它就无法工作，我理解为什么你会不喜欢没有它的东西。
Yes, the full car length is a key design feature. It wouldn't work without it and I understand why you would dislike ones without that.
在 HN 中查看
foxyv 2026/04/07 04:03
在许多地方，骑自行车的人将红灯视为双向停车标志是合法的。

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Idaho_stop

In many locations it is legal for cyclists to treat red lights like a 2 way stop sign.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Idaho_stop

在 HN 中查看
secretsatan 2026/04/07 00:07
我完全赞成这个。拆除的汽车越多，城市就会变得越宜人。虽然我不住在无车城市，但我没有或不需要汽车，当我拥有或需要汽车时，我是汽车共享的会员，可以从当地的超市取车，我可以步行前往。我住在一个多山的城市，虽然我经常走路，但我知道每一个电梯、自动扶梯和偷偷摸摸的公共汽车或地铁可以让我上山，这不是开玩笑，我过去常常步行 20 分钟去上班，那里有 200m 的高度差，但到处都有路。

随着天气逐年变热，这座城市正在加大力度建设绿地、带有喷泉或一些树木的小型城市绿洲，以提供凉爽的场所

I’m all for this. The more cars removed, the more pleasant a city becomes. While i don’t live in a car free city, i don’t own or need a car, when i do, i’m a member of a car share and can just pickup a car from my local supermarket, that i can walk to. I live in a very hilly city, while i walk a lot, i know every lift, escalator and sneaky bus or metro to get me up a hill, it’s no joke here, i used to walk 20min to my job, that had a 200m height difference, but there are paths everywhere.

And the city is working more on greenspaces, little urban oasis’s with a fountain or some trees to provide places to cool down as every year gets hotter

ChrisArchitect 2026/04/07 00:38

巴黎市长拆除停车位并减少汽车数量

Mayor of Paris removed parking spaces, reduced the number of cars
